speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: The 30th of July, 1996, and we're in Sydney, Australia. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Joseph, we're just in the landing-- the D-Day landing at Normandy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part was that where you landed, there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could tell you the exact beach, and the name of the seaside. It was Lion-sur-Mer, and the beach was Sword Beach. People who landed there would know all about it. Sword, that was the name of the actual beach. And the actual Village of a seaside town was Lion-- L-I-O-N-- Lion-sur-Mer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the nearest large town to there? Was-- was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: The large town was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Caen?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Caen. That was about five miles in. But it was so well defended our troops couldn't go in. For about a month we were outside. And when, eventually, our troops took Caen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish troops in the area held a service in a tent. And to their surprise, civilians attended.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they? There were immigrants who left Germany and lived in Caen. When they heard there was a service they came out and joined. Were they in hiding, at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: In hiding, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and did Reverend Harding conduct that service? Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. He was the chaplain.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the chaplain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] did you stay there in that area for very long? And-- or did you move on from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When the troops moved on, we followed them. Wherever they went, we went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of casualties were there? What sort of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the work we did, we were in a special unit. Specially formed. I'll give you an idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the first World War, if any soldier got wounded, they patched him up and sent him back to a hospital. By the time that he reached the hospital, he was dead. Why? he died of shock. So when we, in our war, they devised a method-- that don't send the troops back when they get wounded, just shift them about a mile back, get them-- get them on their feet, then ship them back to hospital. That was our job. So when the troops got wounded, they brought them into our tent. We gave them cigarettes, hot tea, a shot for the shock, yes. And then where were they sent afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, further back to a field hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your unit was really a dressing station? Is that what they called it, a field dressing station? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: FDS, field dressing station.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And the casualties, were they British soldiers, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: British, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there Americans as well, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there weren't any Americans in our area. It was all British. But we had a few Germans. They were treated just the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they were OK, it's on to a hospital or prison camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a chance to talk to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. But what we did notice, that the British troops were friendly with the Germans. They showed each other photographs of their family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were able to communicate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And did you know at this time what was happening in Europe? Did you know about the concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever talk with the other soldiers about what was happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The others weren't interested, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had their own worries, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever know, or-- or-- or try to find out what was happening? You said before, your parents came from Lithuania, and your wife's family came from Europe. Did you ever know anything that was happening with those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know why they left Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but the people who were left behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Disappeared altogether. We didn't know, no. See, my father's [INAUDIBLE], as a boy he had to join the Army. He didn't. They got married, in Kovna, and off they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were still a lot of other relatives back in Europe. There was still a lot of other family back in Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Caen, where did you go after that? Where did the Army go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Paris. When Paris was taken we were allowed in to have a look. All American troops there, only. They didn't want the English troops there. But our officer let us go in, just to have a look around at Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: The French girls. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a lot--

speaker: interviewee
text: They made a [INAUDIBLE] of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a lot of devastation there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a lot of devastation? Did you see a lot of casualties?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you move on again from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were moving all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From there, we crossed the border into Holland. We were stationed in Holland. By the river Maas. I don't' know if you know it. It's part of the Rhine. We were stationed there quite a long time. Nijmegen, there was fighting there. We were outside there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were treating the casualties?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not actual casualties. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, soldiers who were sick, and so on. We were like a followup unit. And from Belgium we were in Holland. I told you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And did you have contact with the Dutch people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you about what was happening in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met quite a few Dutch people, Jewish, who were in hiding. Yes. They were pleased to see me, being Jewish. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to help them in any way? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. See, when you're in the Army, you're tied. You can't go wherever you want to. You go where you're told. And you can't go walking about it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to-- to contact your family at home? Did you write to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we got letters. But we weren't allowed to mention any names. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like places?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, places, nothing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was happening at home, with your wife and your son? What-- where they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the early stage of the war they were evacuated to Cambridge. Out of the bombing area. Then later on, it eased up, they came back. And we lived at home. Well, they did, not me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you were moving all the time. Where did you sleep? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Army? On the ground. I never slept in a bed the whole time I was in the Army. No. We were on the move. If not, all we had was a blanket and a ground sheet, and you slept on that. And glad of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have adequate rations and--

speaker: interviewee
text: All our rations came in tins until our field unit got moving. And they made it-- made-- they did cooking, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And all the time you were only with the other British-- other British soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And coming in, now, to 1945. What happened then, after you'd been in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we crossed over into Germany. And the war apparently was finishing. We never-- we were never in a town. Always in villages, or outside. We finished up in Germany. And war had just finished. I think it was VE-Day.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was May, 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Round about that time. There was victory in Europe. And we were in Germany. And we were celebrating, if you can call it that. About 3 o'clock in the morning, we were sleeping on the ground in the house. We were waken-- woken up-- woken up, and we were told to move on, because the tank car in front of us, they discovered a concentration camp. And there were a lot of typhus patients there-- men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which camp it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Sandbostel.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they couldn't cope with that. They were tank corps. The weren't a military-- uh, what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: A medical unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: A medical unit-- to help out. So we were sent there, 3 o'clock in the morning. We came across this camp. It's a hutted camp. We were told there was about 4,000 men there-- no women. Most of them had typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were dying like flies. Which way could we help? Well, they decided that every patient-- I use the term patient-- had to be cleansed-- washed. The uniform-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it? Clothes they wore had to be destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, typhus is spread by a louse-- lice. And it's very infectious. They had to be burned. And each man had to be washed, and had to be sprayed with anti-louse powder-- that's to kill the lice. A-L-- anti-louse powder. Everybody came-- who came into the camp had to be deloused. Even visitors. We had to be deloused. Before we came amongst the people there, we had to be sprayed-- sprayed with this powder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there-- how many of you were there? There weren't so many in your medical unit to look after all these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was about 25 of us. That's right, very special part of the unit. And we had to watch-- but we couldn't do all that work, there were so many people there. What our commandant did, he sent a message to the nearest town-- I forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bremen. Was it Bremen? Bremen? Was it Bremen, the nearest town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bremen, yes, that's right, Bremen. He wanted 600 women to work in a hospital. An order. See, we were victors. He had to find 600 women and girls to come to work in the hospital. They were brought out to our camp, and there they allocated duties. The strong ones had to dig graves. The others had to the washing. And each one, as they came out of the hutted camp-- it was a filthy space, it was. I had to look inside later on. Had to be placed on a table-- and we supervised that they did their work properly. Everyone had to be washed down and sprayed with powder that killed lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there were any other illnesses, or was it only typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it only typhus, or were there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhus, as far as we knew. There was doctors there, but I didn't know about them. Each one was brought in and cleansed. And when they were cleansed, and if they didn't need any treatment, they were shifted elsewhere. We don't know. Away from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of this place, where you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandbostel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-A-N-D-B-O-S-T-E-L, as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And was that a concentration camp, or was a displaced persons camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was like a staging camp where they brought these inmates-- I use that term, inmates-- from the extermination camps up north. And there they were allowed to remain there, before they were shifted, further still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which camps they came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And as they brought each one in, I stood by the bed, or table, to see that they did their work properly. And as they came in, each one, I whispered to each one. You know what I whispered? [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- did they respond?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But later on I'll tell you, there was a special one. Made me cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can tell me now. Tell me the story now.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a time they brought one in, a man, in his, about, 50s. In his 50s. And there was no room where they'd do the cleansing. He was on a stretcher on the grass. And I knelt down beside him, and I said, [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: He was half stunned beforehand. He didn't know where he was. As soon as I said that, he looked up at me. He said, [YIDDISH]? I said, yes. He bent down and kissed my feet. And who was he? Where did he come from, that man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, where from? He said, Hungary. He said, [YIDDISH]-- Will I live? I said, you'll live, we don't know, we'll try. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was sent out, but I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know. Don't know. You see, they were out of our hands. Once they reached that stage, they were out of our hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any others that you-- you talked to like that? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was one, a young-- youngish man, he was about 35. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: About 35. He spoke English. I said, where from? Lithuania. He came from Kovna. He was a doctor. He had a medical degree, a real doctor. And he knew I spoke-- spoke English. And he said to me, could I do him a favor? He's got cousins in London in the fur trade. Could I get in touch with them, and tell them that he's still alive? I says, I can't contact London at all. It's out of the question. We're in the Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what I'll do, I'll write to my wife and tell her this man has cousins in London who are in the fur trade, mention their name. And just tell them that this doctor is still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Levin. Levin, I think, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the cousins in England, do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember. I forgot. It's over 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to bring them together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But what my wife did, she looked up in the telephone directory of all of the furriers of this particular name. And she found the cousin, and she contacted him, and they wrote. She told me this, that this man had a cousin-- a sister in Canada. And they wrote to the sister, to say that her brother's still alive. That's all I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe he got to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps he did. I don't know. See, once they left us, it was a different story. It didn't concern us any more. We did our job, and off they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me, in that camp, you said you had to burn the clothing. And who provided the new clothing for the people? Who provided the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. See, we did our job, and we were finished. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Another little incident, they brought in two little boys, Jewish boys, they must have been about 10 years old. And I spoke Yiddish to them. And they said, could I help him-- help them? With what? They were twins. They'd been-- two boys had been together all the time. Now, when they go away from our-- our camp, would I see they were kept together? I says, I'll try my best, I can't tell. Once you're finished with us, I'll speak to the officer. I'll tell him to keep the two boys together. And that's all you know about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all we know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And did anybody-- any of the soldiers, did they take photographs? Or do you know of anybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't allowed to have cameras. No. Because in case we were captured, and the Germans-- well, knew exactly where we were. We weren't allowed to keep-- keep a diary.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And you said there were a lot of people in that camp. I suppose many of them died, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there? Did you witness the burials? Did you see what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I saw the German women digging graves. That's all I saw. And I walked into one of the camps, it was a hutted. There was three banks, and they were lying the inmates-- I'm sorry I use that term-- they were lying all on top of each other. Just in a filthy state, and they had typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their bowels were working and running. And what did they do? They couldn't get off the camp. Wherever they were, they used it. It was all over the floor, yes. And were there rabbis there, to-- to perform--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the graves weren't marked in any way--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: All we saw was digging the graves, [BACKGROUND NOISE] but that's about all. We don't know. See, we were in a field unit, with no facilities for anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were you only with-- you were only with the British soldiers. You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't come in contact with the American soldiers at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Americans were a bit more down south. We were north. Going towards Hamburg, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And were you able to communicate with those German women who came in to do that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: The women, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we had to do, as they washed each one down, all they were-- the inmates-- skin and bone. You saw their chest, like a bird cage covered with skin. They [INAUDIBLE] thin. And we had to demonstrate to the German women, see, this man is a normal man-- he was. Look at him now, a skeleton. Thank Hitler for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they respond to that? Well, most of them say they didn't know. Could be true, I don't know. But we had one girl there, she was about 16, and she was a very good-- a German girl-- very good worker, very conscientious. And when she saw these men, she told us she belonged to the Hitler Youth. She said you couldn't help herself, because they enjoy themselves. All the girls and boys, it was good. But what she did, she tore her card up. And because you could speak Yiddish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were able to communicate with them in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them speak English at all? Or-- the Germans that you dealt with there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I know. It was only like, hardly a village, just a few houses there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the-- what did the English soldiers say when they saw what had happened to these Jews? Did they talk about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did talk about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Because it was brought home to us in actual facts. Up to now it was just theory-- hearsay. But now we saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and how did you feel, being the only Jew there in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt sad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Thinking that my people came from Lithuania, and if they didn't get married, they would have been there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that affect you for a long time after the war, too? Did you used to think about that a lot, and did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to think about it a lot. But it's only thoughts, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was brought home to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have any contact with any of those people after the war? Did any of them come to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all disappeared. No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in Germany for quite some time, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before the end of the war in Japan.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the unit that I was with was an invasion unit, trained for invasion. Now, the war in Japan was still on. But in Europe it was finished. They-- the government decided to bring our unit back to England, to fit us out with new clothes for-- for jungle warfare. And we were going for the invasion of the Japanese landings.

speaker: interviewer
text: They brought us back to-- Belgium, a place called Ghent. I don't know if you know it. Do you know it? Ghent? It's a town-- a well-known town.

speaker: interviewee
text: A town, yes. We were there, and we were going to be brought back to England to be fitted out. But then the bomb dropped in Japan.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was August-- in August.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they brought us back to Germany again. So I was saved for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what part of Germany did you go back to? Where were you in Germany? Did you go back to Bremen, around that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were only in villages. A place called [? Mettigan, ?] I think. A place you never heard of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then? How long did you stay in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it until 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, one time we were stationed in a castle, a schloss. The baron still lived there, but he was pro-British, so they say. We were allowed to live there. Not any best rooms, not a ballroom. In the out houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you discharged from the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see, we all had-- they worked your-- they worked out the time you were going out by your age and in the amount of service you did in the forces. I was number 29. But I forget when it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it 1946 already, by the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And-- and were you de-briefed at all? Did they try to talk to you about the horror that you'd seen in the camp there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one talked to you about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were just [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you went back to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went back to London, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you talk about your experiences when you got back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you talk to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people, I mean, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted to know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked to other Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And did you go back into the fur trade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I couldn't stop in the fur trade. Do you know why? I was used to the outdoor life. Living outdoors all the time. And I couldn't go back into a factory. I couldn't stand in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took a job selling. Selling, and I could be outside, that's right. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how I finished up.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a sales--

speaker: interviewee
text: Working for [? Costetna. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK that's the end of tape two of the interview with Joseph Abrahams. It's the 30th of July, 1996 and we're in Sydney, Australia.
